title: At-Home-Advisor
pros: The position is at home. The company is AMAZING. There are limitless advancement opportunities. You work with some very cool people and the leadership cares about your development. You may get coaching but you never get battered or belittled. The pay is decent and the benefits include, 401(k) match, stock purchase options, product discounts and discounts on services across many different areas, education assistance, child care assistance, paid vacation, sick time and other time off options, health club Reimbursment or bike cost set off. You get 1.5 time for OT and it's pretty much unlimited as long as you don't exceed 12 hours in a day or 59 total in a week.
cons: The schedule changes every 90 days and you have to bid for your shifts so you never know what you are going to get. This makes it extremely hard to plan your life to far in advance. You are also pretty much assured to work a Saturday or Sunday.
advice_to_management: The management consists of a team manager and their area manager. Meetings are held over video and chat. They are supportive of individual development and help you to reach your goal. Management will challenge you if you want to advance but it's always in the spirit of making you better to prepare you for the next role.

title: Changing the world (in a small way)
pros: Working at Apple means that the things you work on get into the hands of hundreds of millions of people, literally. It's a lot of pressure to get things right and drives one to do the best work of their lives. 2+ years later I'm still blown away by the talent and creativity of people I work with.
cons: It's a lot of work, teams are usually strapped for resources and under pressure to deliver on strict timelines leading to high stress and long hours at certain times during the year.
advice_to_management: Keep fighting for "what's right". Renewable energy, civil liberties, working condition, everything. I couldn't be prouder of that aspect of the company, in the end it's about a lot more than profits and earnings.

title: Least Evil of the FAANG companies
pros: After about 6 months in contract, it is clear that Apple, for the most part, is making a good-faith effort to treat me and my coworkers well.
cons: My lead is a "superstar". A pleasure to work with and genuinely brilliant. The down-side is that they're veeeeery busy. I do not sense that Apple intended this as a "throw 'em in and see if they swim" position. But because of my lead's limited availability there's been a lot that I've had to figure out myself. At least one of my coworkers had a similar experience, being with Apple almost a year before getting properly ramped up to speed.
advice_to_management: Keep treating your employees well. Apple does need to be better with on-boarding new employees. Because so much of the source code is internal, we can't figure stuff out by just googling it. Do not rush into the AI bandwagon (so far you haven't). Apple is better off making an informed decision on it's AI strategy AFTER the AI bubble has popped. (...and it WILL pop.)

title: Good job, but extremely stressful
pros: Apple does really focus on a work life balance and allows you to take a lot of time to yourself if needed (and approved).
cons: Complete and total burnout is expected. Data Center logistics can be good, but expect a LOT to be put on your plate. I cannot count the amount of projects that we (or just me) would be handed and told next to nothing about how to accomplish it. It was an extremely, "sink or swim" place that, I think, THEY thought made you stronger, but in reality it just makes you question why you're going at 150% at all times for the salary you are making.
advice_to_management: They need to hire more people and stop thinking that their small teams can handle the extreme loads they already have.

title: Great Benefits, Good place to work depending on dept
pros: Apple is still an incredible company with world-class talent, strong benefits, and the opportunity to work on products and systems that operate at massive global scale. The people on the ground—engineers, operators, program managers—are deeply committed, smart, and mission-driven. When teams are empowered, the impact can be incredibly rewarding.
cons:
Within IS&T the recent wave of reorganizations has been deeply discouraging. Roles and responsibilities have shifted frequently, often without clear strategy or long-term vision. Strong contributors have been moved into positions they did not want or were never designed for, while leadership promotions increasingly appear disconnected from operational experience or proven impact.
There is also a growing sense of cultural narrowing at the leadership level. Decision-making and advancement seem to favor a very specific leadership profile and way of thinking, which limits diversity of perspective and can stifle healthy debate and innovation. As a result, many experienced employees feel stuck, undervalued, or uncertain about their future despite years of strong performance.
advice_to_management: Stability matters. Invest more in transparent career paths, reward demonstrated results over optics, and broaden what “good leadership” looks like. Apple succeeds when it values diverse viewpoints and empowers people closest to the work. Re-orgs should enable teams—not exhaust them.

title: Great experience, but not what it used to be
pros:
Incredible opportunity to work on the world's best products and most of the people really care about the product. It was a lot of fun to see how the world's most admired company works behind the scenes. If you have a good manager, you can go pretty far, pretty quick.

Compensation and PTO is very fair for Silicon Valley. The culture is very design and user experience driven which may seem obvious, but you should feel the same way before joining. Sometimes sacrifices in the technical implementation are made because it provides a better user design.
cons:
My main complaint is that it gets repetitive after a few years, specifically working on the OS side, you just feel like it's the same cycle-driven work year in and year out. Work life balance can be tough, especially leading up to major OS releases.

The hybrid schedule is nice, but the in office days force you to commute to/from Cupertino to sit in online meetings. I put a low score for DEI because there is not a huge representation of underserved minorities in tech. That becomes apparent from day one on campus, despite what the values are of the company.
advice_to_management:
The obvious thing here is to stop making your engineers who have successful track records come into the office. It is a waste of time and the old Apple "water cooler" culture is dead. Over the years, I have seen some exceptional engineers and people walk out due to this aging policy that has a tremendous influence on the industry.

Not a lot of leadership from underrepresented (in-tech) groups which is discouraging given the public perception of the company in the marketing.

title: awesome
pros:
Pros
Brand prestige: Working at Apple instantly boosts your résumé. It opens doors in tech, marketing, design, and even unrelated industries.

Culture of excellence: You’re surrounded by sharp, detail-obsessed people. The standards are sky-high, which can push you to grow faster than anywhere else.

Product ecosystem perks: Deep discounts on Apple products and early exposure to new tech. Employees genuinely get excited about launches.

Strong benefits: Excellent healthcare, stock purchase plan, and paid time off. Full-time employees often feel financially secure.

Training and structure: Whether you’re in a store or corporate, training is thorough. Apple’s onboarding and communication systems are elite.

Diversity and inclusion (mostly real): Company-wide emphasis on diversity, though it varies by location or team.

Cool factor: People light up when you say you work at Apple. There’s real social capital attached to the brand.
cons:
Cons
Workload and intensity: The “Apple perfectionism” culture means you’ll work long hours or feel like you should. Burnout is common, especially in retail and engineering.

Micromanagement: Leadership can be rigid. There’s a “this is how Apple does it” mindset that leaves little room for personal style or flexibility.

Internal secrecy: You often can’t discuss projects, even with coworkers. Communication silos make collaboration weirdly difficult for such a huge company.

Limited upward mobility (retail): It’s hard to move from retail to corporate, no matter how talented you are. Many employees feel stuck unless they leave and reapply elsewhere.

Culture of fear: Mistakes are magnified. Some managers use pressure instead of mentorship. It’s not a “safe to fail” environment.

Corporate politics: Promotions and recognition sometimes depend on who you know, not just what you produce.

Retail schedules: If you’re in-store, expect constant weekends and holiday shifts. “Work-life balance” depends entirely on your manager.

title: Make a Difference for our Senior Community- Great Benefits- High Stess
pros: A Culture of Connection and Innovation Apple provides an environment where the "human element" is truly prioritized in the customer journey. The commitment to Steps of Service allows Specialists to go beyond simple transactions and build genuine rapport. Additionally, the peer-to-peer support is exceptional; working alongside highly skilled Technicians and fellow Specialists creates a collaborative atmosphere where everyone is trying to deliver excellence despite operational hurdles. It is a fast-paced, high-energy environment that rewards those who thrive on problem-solving and authentic connection.
cons: Operational Strains and Leadership Visibility Gaps The primary challenge lies in the disconnect between "peak volume" and "leadership presence." During high-traffic periods, there is often a lack of visible management on the floor, leaving Specialists and Leads to navigate escalations, overrides, and sales guidance without adequate support. This often results in "vague promises" to customers (e.g., "someone will be with you momentarily") rather than actual service. When managers remain in back-office admin roles during these critical windows, it places an immense burden on the frontline team, leading to burnout and a feeling of being "stretched beyond capacity."
advice_to_management:
Lead by Example with a "Triage" Mindset To elevate store efficiency, leadership should adopt a more proactive, triage-inspired approach during peak hours. When the floor is at capacity, managers shouldn't just be "visible", they should be active participants in the initial Steps of Service.  Active Discovery: Instead of telling a customer "it will be a moment," initiate the "Group Discovery" or "Probing" steps to begin a bag for the Specialist. This mirrors a healthcare intake process and ensures the customer feels "seen" immediately.

Structured Rotations: Implement clearer structures for when managers transition from admin tasks to floor support.

Accessibility: Ensure there is always a clearly designated leader on the floor who is not solely tied to a single customer interaction, but available to support the team with overrides and escalations. Modeling the behavior you want to see will significantly boost team morale and operational flow.

title: Lots of potential, but stuck in the past
pros: Excellent offices, good cafe, good pay, decent work life balance, interesting problems to solve, if you can get to do them.
cons: Extreme (and largely useless) secrecy makes it difficult to get anything done. The "Apple way" of doing things is by plucking some people from separate teams that specialize in something and sort of mushing  them together as a virtual team. This has its advantages and disadvantages. Advantage: those teams can really deeply specialize in a narrow sub-field, and as such deliver insights and results that generalists can't deliver. You can see this in Apple products every day. Disadvantage: this works only on the hardware side. On the software side, I would much rather have teams of capable generalists working on products, but with the ability (and requirement) to reach out to specialists when needed. That'd work way better than "Apple way" works today.
advice_to_management: Consider seriously reorganizing the way you work on software, especially on AI software. Consider allowing the internal use of AI coding and code review aids. I'm a believer in Apple, and seeing how the sausage is made there only bolstered my beliefs. There are lots of people there who really care. It'd be a shame if Apple permanently fell off the wagon, which it might if it doesn't adapt.

title: Good managers, great company culture, poor hourly wage.
pros: Great managers. Supportive leaders who are invested in your development and growth. No micromanagement from my team leaders, managers or senior leaders. Good teamwork and teammates. Very little pressure and very little gossiping. No bullying or toxic interactions amongst teammates or leaders. No work drama. Respectful of private lives of employees. Great at respecting work life balance. Great at diversity and inclusion efforts. Very inclusive company that has great integrity from the top down. HR and management are great at respecting and empowering employees to use the disability accommodations and accessiblility needs that they need to succeed within the workforce. Most people in the line of work I’m in have great emotional intelligence as well has high technical savvy. My leadership team has been great at handling conflict and addressing feedback given to them. Mostly great health benefits, Great campus, wonderful services, and discounts. Fun job. Great company values and ethics! Great clubs and social groups and community within the company. Apple was great at supporting employees during Covid with no layoffs. Great opportunities for career and personal development such as the mentor program, the career experience, and rotations. Leaders are supportive of staff when clients are abusive.
cons: Low hourly salary in comparison to competitors while expecting higher quality work, more technical experience, higher capacity workload with less downtime. Mismatch in expectations from vender/ contractor employees that do not perform at the level of regular staff. This creates frustration due to poor performance and an inconsistent experience for clients and staff. Difficult to level up as their is a ceiling in my line of business for promotions, unless you want to be a manager. Hard / impossible to give feedback to venders and contractors to improve consistency. Cap of vacation days is 21-22 days after 10 years. As a global company we have fewer vacation days than most other modern countries, we also have fewer paid sick days. Our employees only get recognition for tenure at 5, 10, 20 years in the form of an adjustment in vacation hours and a sculpture or plaque, instead of monetary compensation, trips or spiffs to help the employee celebrate these great milestones and achievements. Raises, benefits and rewards in recent years have not kept up with inflation and cost of living adjustments. Employee spiffs and rewards for performance have become less frequent and have decreased in value. Employees are given only 1-2 hours a month to spend on personal development. Employees are expected to spend less than 30 seconds between calls to refresh and prepare for the next client. There is not a true “Pre Sales” manager escalation role so Senior Sales Experts have to act as a quasi manager without having much power. Employees who are cross trained are not encouraged to use those skills, abilities and tools to help expedite the customer experience while at the same time Not paying employees more to be cross trained and then utilizing them for that reason whenever the business needs require. Also Senior Sales Experts are underutilized for the skills the team has while also not getting paid enough for the experience and knowledge they offer.
advice_to_management: Leadership. Please pay everyone more. We are not keeping up with inflation. We need to afford to live. We need more time off, more flexibility in schedules to encourage better working conditions, rest and reduce burno ur(4 day work week with 32 hours caps would be amazing, while adjusting hourly pay to keep the annual gross income the same as a 40 hour 5 day work week)  increase in vacation accrual caps a year (21-22 days for 10year employees to 26-30) in order to match global leave standards) and then increasing employees annual available sick days. Also changing the attendance policy to be more fair and easier to understand.

title: The Worst Experience I've Ever Had as a Contractor
pros:
*The pay is decent for a contract role

*People in this contract position are allowed to work remotely a couple days a week, same as the Full-Time Employees (FTEs)

*Management is generally flexible with hours and giving time off if you request it (though bear in mind any kind of PAID time off wasn’t an option, even if everyone is out of the office regardless because it’s a holiday)
cons: *The relatively high (generally) hourly wage offered may seem enticing but I cannot in good conscience recommend this role to any experienced professional. The general vibe for contractors in this role seems to be that you feel very disposable and are not treated as important at all. Even during the interview process, I was ghosted twice: two times the Apple interviewer did not show up and left me sitting there having wasted 30 minutes of my time, and when the interviews were rescheduled they offered no apology or explanation. A lot of what they told me during the interview also seemingly turned out to not be the case at all: When I asked about onboarding, training, and general resources, I was told they had several onboarding resources and training and support and was assured I would not have a lot thrown at me at all at once. On my second day my boss was throwing multiple projects at me and I had to use my boss’ computer to do it because I still didn’t even have permissions set up and I was even given her password so I could log back in at her computer while my boss was in meetings, which I’m not sure is actually even acceptable company policy. I never had any free time to look at any of the internal wikis or documentation (which were largely out of date anyway) and at one point I asked another contractor who had been here for nearly a year if their experience was similar and they mentioned they didn’t think they had so much as looked at any of the resources in their entire tenure so far. (The same peer also told me their advice to me was that if I wanted to last here it also probably wasn’t a good idea to ask too many questions, which says a lot about the environment.) Furthermore, a lot of the internal tools we used were broken and it was also apparently it was my job to QA the tools for the engineers while I’m using them which added an extra layer of stress. So my experience was completely contrary to what I was told in the interviews, pretty much a sink or swi
advice_to_management:
I seriously doubt management will listen to any feedback any contractor tells them as they made no attempt to give me any kind of feedback, be it positive OR constructive, during my contract period and when it came time to set me loose they did so without warning, and generally did not uphold any of the standards or promises they claimed to set in the interview, much less during my time there.

On that note, however, there is one thing I can at least try to give them advice on: in the future, during the interview process, the least you can do is be honest with potential contract hires about what they are getting into from the start. In the interviews, do NOT tell potential hires that the team environment is welcoming and “we’re all friends” when that seems to only apply to the FTEs and meanwhile the contract staff is slammed, exhausted, and working at their desks in silence and misery all day. Do NOT tell people that you have a solid onboarding plan and a bunch of internal documentation and wikis for helpful resources and that you won’t throw a bunch of stuff at them all at once, and then when they actually start, completely ignore the established 2 week training and onboarding curriculum, give your hires no time to read the documentation you claim to have, and instead right from the start have them start managing multiple projects on their second day using a computer that belongs to their manager with credentials you don’t have set up yet, tools that don’t work, and only vague instructions to go on, and then blame your contractor when they aren’t getting things right the first time because you failed to provide any sort of the training or foundation you said you would. Do NOT tell them you “welcome feedback” only to refuse to provide anything other than a very basic overview of each extremely detailed task you throw at them in rapid succession and otherwise do not regularly check in with or manage your contract staff at all, and instead be clear that if there are any even suspected issues with them, performance or otherwise, that they will be let go immediately and without any kind of warning or feedback or conversation at all, because contractors here apparently have no rights and you can replace them easily. 

I would recommend instead to be honest and upfront from the start: tell prospective hires that this is a “sink or swim” environment that has a lot of mental and psychological stressors, everyone is busy and stressed out, the FTEs are too busy to actually manage you or bother to give you any sort of real onboarding or foundation, and that as a contractor there will be no feedback or warnings given and that you can be let go and replaced very easily. Would any of this paint a positive image for prospective new contract LPM hires about what working here is like? Probably not. But would you at least be telling them honestly what they’re getting into? Yes, absolutely. You can at least do that much.

cons:
- Too much "drinking the kool aid". WAY too much. I hope this is an apt metaphor: Imagine you have an 8oz cup in your hand. They pour a 64oz pitcher of Kool-Aid into your 8oz. cup. Then they pour a pitcher of sugar into the same cup. You get diabetes. The amount of company propaganda is absolutely astounding and excessive to point of being absolutely ridiculous, wherein you feel like a caricature of yourself wearing this blue shirt. This is coming from me, a lifelong Apple fan since 1987 (the year I started using computers).
- FAR too much bashing of anything that isn't Apple. Apple has now decided as a company, to compare itself to Samsung and Google, etc etc. It used to decide not to compare itself to anybody. This shows everywhere from development to retail attitudes.
- Willing isolation from and ignorance of the rest of the world of technology. Apple exists in a bubble, and those within the bubble are blissfully unaware that any other technology is relevant and useful. I heard one employee say that Microsoft Office is going the way of the dinosaurs. Nobody called him out on this. What dream world is he living in???? Isn't it better to learn Apple's technology in a way that helps it workably coexist with other technology? This is equally as important as learning the Apple ecosystem, which, yes, in itself is amazing.
- Training is too general, not specific enough to help sharpen one's technical skill set on a consistent basis.
- No ongoing training program to keep your technical skills sharp and up to date. Instead, they offer "Pathways", which is meant to improve your social skills. Great! But isn't that why they hired us to begin with? You have to search Apple's technical reporting system for relevant known issues, which is fine but if you don't (and a lot of techs don't)  you usually find out what those are when a customer has a problem and you don't know the answer. One person who happened to look at iDesk might know the answer, but if they're not availabl
advice_to_management:
My feedback section was long, I realize, but it's because I genuinely care about Apple as a company and would love to see things improve in real, tangible ways where the signal to noise ratio is considered for retail as much as it is considered for its products and services!

Consider and analyze the cons I wrote above and communicate them with your market leaders! How successful IS Pathways, really, for example? Everything has to be given a chance to work, of course, but I think maybe corporate doesn't get enough constructive feedback from its retail stores for fear of being perceived as not having put enough effort into making these things work.

Regardless, I loved working for Apple and would absolutely return given the need and/or opportunity.

title: Not what it used to be, but you'll definitely grow
pros: Great benefits, incredible experiences, you can purse many interests and learn a lot. Depending on where you enter the company (and your leadership team) it can truly change your life.
cons: Most entry-level or mid-level people should realistically expect a maximum of 2 significant raises/promotions across your time with the company unless you exit and come back in a higher role (called a boomerang). The pace of work can be very manic, especially if you are in a global facing role. Expect to find yourself investing heavily in work that will either never materialize as a complete end-product or be compromised significantly to the point that it doesn't deliver the promised ROI. Not long ago Apple still had tons of start-up level energy but now has taken on the same political infighting and administrative burden that handicap most well-intentioned technology companies.
advice_to_management: Set 3-5 year strategies instead of following an annual capital plan that really only allows for ~6 months of "deep" work. All capital planning can ladder up to the "big rocks" making it more meaningful. Stop grinding your talent through the meat grinder and get rid of VP level folks who have been promoted past their ability to perform and do more harm than good with their clique based cultures.

title: Some Orgs are better than others
pros:
- Depending on your org this will be different.  IS&T (specifically the helpline branch and it's offshoots) is the org I will be reviewing. But doesn't represent the company as a whole. With that said, here are some Pros.

- Discounts on products, although this is all of Apple.
- I've met some good friends here
cons:
Remember this is org specific, other orgs may give you a better experience.

- This team is managed by former retail store managers who are non technical. Essentially meaning they are there to just watch over your shoulder constantly.  There is no value add.
- Going back to the former Retail aspect.  Retail has a very "fake" culture. This team has adopted this because leadership.
- Nepotism runs rampant here.  If you don't know someone you will not get ahead, Plain and simple.  People get advanced with no technical skills yet they just happen to have a connection.
-Lack of any of kind of empathy or people skills with some managers. You could literally be dying and they'd ask if you could come in to work. Zero awareness.
- Raises are not a guarantee here (although that can be said in other orgs. But it's especially true here)
-Dealing with a lot of mental health sufferers. I've never seen a job cause so much depression and anxiety for people. Most people put on fake smiles but a lot of them want out. Some saying transferring to the org was the biggest career move mistake they've made.
- I don't think Apple on your resume carries as much weight anymore.
- Although you are answering calls, Hybrid onsite  is required. 3 times a week 
- Lack of true opportunity or development. You can ask for chances to learn from other teams but managers will always say it's on you, even though you don't get any time to do so. (Unless you're a nepotism hire that is).
- Very bad work-life balance, good luck getting PTO approved.
advice_to_management:
If you read this I doubt you'll care

1. Allow your directs to grow and help then grow.
2. Quit with the nepotism/favoritism/ cronyism , People see it and it is obvious. It kills morale and causes issues.


As for senior management, you never interact with entry level to middle management so you have no idea what goes on.

title: Genius Bar Team
pros: Constant learning: Apple hardware, iOS, macOS and services - Technician builds strong foundational tech skills (diagnostic, troubleshooting and customer education; Customer Interaction & Soft Skills - it improves communication, empathy, and problem-solving under pressure and good for building a career in customer success, technical support, or management; Structured Environment - clear procedure, training and tools for most scenarios and access to internal documentation and training that deepen technician's tech knowledge; Career Path Opportunity - good stepping stone to roles like Genius (repair tech), Creative (trainer), management or corporate support roles and last one, Variety in Daily Work - Mix of troubleshooting, teaching, and (light) sales & technician sees many different issues and customer
cons: High pressure & Customer stress - technician often deals with upset people (data loss, broken phones, or urgent issues). Remain stay calm and empathetic even when customers are unfair or emotional. Repetitive Issues - Many problems are similar (forgot Apple Account [Apple ID] password, broken screen and no backups. Can feel repetitive and mentally tiring over time. Retail constraints - Schedule often includes weekends, evenings and holidays and standing on your feet for long periods in a busy and loud store. Limited technical depth (in-store) - technician's doing front-line troubleshooting and triage, not deep engineering and it complexes repairs or advanced diagnostics go to Geniuses or off-site repair centers, which can feel limiting if technical want very technical work. Metrics and targets - It may be measured on customer satisfaction, resolution time, and sometimes sales-related metrics (attachments like AppleCare+). It can create pressure to move quickly even when technician'd like more time with each customer. Strict policies - most follow Apple policies on repairs, replacements and exceptions, even when technician personally want to "just help" more and limited flexibility can be frustrating in edge cases.
advice_to_management:
It's important to remember that I can frame my advice as helping TS (Technical Specialist) be more effective that keeps it positive and professional. Better balance between speed, sales and real solutions. It's balancing speed, customer satisfaction and sales. Sometimes the pressure to move quickly or focus on attachments makes it harder to fully solve technical issues. If we could slight relax pressure on complex cases, we could prevent repeat visits and improve long-term customer satisfaction. 

It makes it easier to see how to grow (Genius, Creative, management, corporate and et cetera. Smarter scheduling and staffing - reduce burnout and chaos at peak times. 

Emotional and customer-interaction support - handling upset customers is a big part of the job; support matters. A lot of our work is emotional labor - helping frustrated or stressed customers. More training in de-escalation and more explicit support from leaders when we face difficult situations would go a long way. Even short debriefs after really tough interactions would help. 

Recognize quality of service, not just numbers that reward real problem-solving and teaching, not only metrics. It would be motivating if recognition and feedback looked not only at metrics like speed and attachments, but also at the quality of our technical problem-solving and how well we educate customers. That's what really builds loyalty and reduces future issues.

title: Channel retail has been run into the ground
pros: Honestly Apple as a company is great to work for. Great stock options, better than avg pay compared to other companies if you’re in a retail setting.the people really make the job worth sticking around for. I’ve met some of the most intelligent/innovative people in line level positions.
cons:
Channel retail (formerly field sales/channel sales) is a shell of an org from what it was. It used to be run by Steve cano - who now works for Levi Jeans. Current leadership has managed to destroy the culture, personality, and more than likely long term growth of the program. The line level sales people in channel were pillars of their locations, trusted advisors to partner management and paid well enough that looking for another job made no sense. The current setup is a conveyor belt of hapless new hires who are more inept than the last, due in part to the decrease in hiring standards. Associates used to be paid commission for driving revenue in stores, this went away because leadership couldn’t figure out how to build a proper compensation plan. For a company that has a 30% margin, and the highest ASP in their class skipping out on your soul, the people is an insane move. 

They used to allow RMs to manage their region with ultimate flexibility, as they understood their markets better than anyone. This autonomy went away, to be managed by 1%ers who don’t understand the avg person that they serve. Everyone who joins, is looking for a way out, unless they’re so far deep (stock, grandfathered pay etc) that they just accept they’re going down with the ship. The brain drain that has occurred doesn’t even allow Halfway decent people to move up, because THERE IS NO PROMOTION OPPORTUNITY. Leads don’t exist, RMs are hired from external or brought over from retail (out of their depth) as opposed to people who have worked for years to build the channel they want to lead. As channel retail has become more visible to the larger corporate org, the worse it’s gotten. We used to have yearly sales conferences where we train, and engage with our colleagues. There was always something you could take away from these meetings. Sure it costs money, but again, you’re talking about the most profitable company on the planet. We competed with every other consumer brand that had a presence
advice_to_management: Find your soul, the people will make you money 100x over if they’re not looking for a way out.
